coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots/curcumin nanocomposite for combined Photodynamic/photothermal dual-mode antibacterial therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Fangzhou
Li, Peiyuan
Meng, Hongrong
Yan, Hongjun
Huang, Xiaodong
Cui, Hao
Su, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Due to their excellent photophysical properties, carbon quantum dots have great potential in biomedical and drug delivery fields. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with good water solubility were prepared using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors, and compounded with curcumin, a photosensitive component, to produce composite nanomaterials with photodynamic therapy and synergistic photothermal therapy. The formation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and composite nanomaterials was verified using physical and optical means. In addition, the composite nanomaterials produced single-linear oxygen and exacerbated the increase of solution temperature under blue (405 nm) and near-infrared (808 nm) light irradiation, respectively. The plate counting method showed that the composite nanomaterials exhibited good photodynamic synergistic photothermal antibacterial properties against both Gram-negative Escherichia coli and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus under dual light source (405+808 nm) irradiation, which improved the photoinactivation of curcumin against bacteria. In addition, the composite nanomaterials exhibited low toxicity and good hemocompatibility. These experimental results suggested that the composite nanomaterials showed great potential in a multimodal photodynamic therapy synergistic photothermal treatment platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Photodynamic therapy
Photothermal therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) CDs and Cur, CDs-Cur UV absorption spectra (B) CDs emission fluorescence spectra (C) CDs-Cur emission fluorescence spectra (D) CDs and Cur, CDs-Cur IR spectra.
(A)DPBF+ H2O, (B) DPBF +Cur, (C) DPBF+ CDs-Cur and (D) H2O, Cur, CDs-Cur at 405 nm light source graphs of irradiation degradation DPBF.
(A) Temperature changes of CDs, and CDs-Cur after illumination. (B) Temperature change diagram of CDs-Cur water solution after repeated water solution photograph.

(A) Pictures of LB agar plates of different treatments of S. aureus and E. coli. Survival of E. coli after incubation with different concentrations of Cur and CDs-Cur: (B) dark treatment, (C) 405 nm and 808+405 nm irradiation. Survival of S. aureus after incubation with different concentrations of Cur and CDs-Cur: (D) dark treatment, (E) 405 nm and 808+405 nm irradiation. Values are mean ± standard deviation (SD) (n = 3)

CDs-Cur biocompatibility evaluation: (A) hemolysis rate of erythrocytes after incubation with different concentrations of CDs-Cur; (B) cell survival rate after treatment with different concentrations of CDs-Cur

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots/curcumin nanocomposite for combined Photodynamic/photothermal dual-mode antibacterial therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmacy, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Pharmacy, Guangxi University of Chinese Medicine, Nanning, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bGuangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their excellent photophysical properties, carbon quantum dots have great potential in biomedical and drug delivery fields. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with good water solubility were prepared using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors, and compounded with curcumin, a photosensitive component, to produce composite nanomaterials with photodynamic therapy and synergistic photothermal therapy. The formation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and composite nanomaterials was verified using physical and optical means. In addition, the composite nanomaterials produced single-linear oxygen and exacerbated the increase of solution temperature under blue (405 nm) and near-infrared (808 nm) light irradiation, respectively. The plate counting method showed that the composite nanomaterials exhibited good photodynamic synergistic photothermal antibacterial properties against both Gram-negative Escherichia coli and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus under dual light source (405+808 nm) irradiation, which improved the photoinactivation of curcumin against bacteria. In addition, the composite nanomaterials exhibited low toxicity and good hemocompatibility. These experimental results suggested that the composite nanomaterials showed great potential in a multimodal photodynamic therapy synergistic photothermal treatment platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Many diseases are caused by bacterial infections, yet the rate of discovery of new antibiotics has not kept pace with the emergence of bacterial resistance problems, hence the desire to develop novel antimicrobial technologies. In recent decades, phototherapy techniques that combine exogenous photosensitizers and light for non-antibiotic inactivation of microorganisms have emerged [1], including photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy. PDT gets rid of the chemical damage caused by high temperature and relies on photosensitive reaction, i.e. reactive oxygen species (ROS) is generated by the joint action of three factors: photosensitizer, light source and oxygen, and ROS has strong oxidizing property which can destroy the structure of microorganisms or malignant tumor cells, thus achieving the killing effect [2,3]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) relies on photothermal agents to convert light into energy and penetrate tissues through local irradiation in the near infrared to achieve protein denaturation and irreversible bacterial destruction to achieve the killing effect [4–6]. As seen from the above two mechanisms, combining PDT with PTT may be a more effective antibacterial strategy: when the temperature increases under the light irradiation, the increased permeability of the bacterial cell membrane promotes the absorption of the photosensitizer, while the reactive oxygen species makes the bacterial activity decrease, which is more conducive to the photothermal effect [7–9].

Photosensitizers are key factors in photodynamic antimicrobial activity. Porphyrins as a generation of photosensitizers have been widely investigated and applied in cancer treatment [10,11], ion detection [12], agricultural insecticide [13], etc., however, the poor stability and long clearance time in vivo and strict protection from light for a long time after treatment limit their application [14]; 5-Aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) represents the second generation of photosensitizers with shortened clearance time, which has been further used in skin diseases [15,16], arthritis treatment [17], etc., but there are still toxic side effects that can cause serious organism damage. The third generation of new photosensitizers based on nanotechnology has been gradually developed with the advantages of significant efficacy and less toxic side effects.

Carbon nanodots, also known as carbon quantum dots (CDs), are a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with carbon as the main component, which have good biocompatibility and stable fluorescence properties and are widely used in various fields such as bioimaging [18,19], anti-tumor and anti-cancer [20,21], and photocatalysis [22,23]. Studies have shown that carbon quantum dots as excellent carrier materials, are favorable for drug loading through their high surface area and volume ratio. Moreover, rich amphiphilicity of functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, and to some extent, optimize drug solubility and reduce toxicity. Li et al [24]. proposed a HA-modified carbon dots prepared with citric acid, branched polyethylene glycol amine and hyaluronic acid and loaded with DOX to synthesize a nanoparticle drug delivery platform (HA-CD@p-CBA-DOX). The complexes reduce the in vivo toxicity of DOX and have good biocompatibility, which also enhances the accumulation in tumor tissues and has stronger antitumor activity compared to free DOX. On the other hand, carbon quantum dots can act as antibacterial agents together with drugs by doping the surface functionalization of nitrogen and sulfur atoms, promoting the production of reactive oxygen species and enhancing bacterial interactions. Dhermendra K. Tiwarir [25] used antimicrobial mathematical modeling to find that CDs and tetracycline synergistically enhanced the antimicrobial activity against E. coli at concentrations 10 times lower than the MIC as well. Similarly, AgNPs@S,N-CQds composites having AgNPs naturally surrounded by S,N-CQD possessed more efficient antibacterial properties than each monomeric material through the production of ROS [26],.

Curcumin, a natural photosensitizer with powerful antibacterial properties, is a class of polyphenolic compounds extracted from the rhizome of the ginger plant Turmeric [27]. Studies have shown that curcumin can inhibit the formation of bacterial biofilms and enhance the body's ability to clear bacteria, and is often used in the treatment of bacterial diseases [28,29]. However, curcumin is insoluble in water, which affects its photostability and limits its photodynamic antibacterial applications. We hypothesized that taking advantage of carbon quantum dot carriers to improve the solubility and bioavailability of curcumin would be beneficial to obtain more efficient antibacterial properties and promote drug efficacy. Therefore, in this thesis, we selected citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors and synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots by microwave method to load curcumin and construct composite nanomaterials. It is expected that the loading of carbon quantum dots will improve the antibacterial performance of curcumin, and the composite nanomaterials with good biocompatibility can be used for PDT and PTT combination therapy with high efficiency. This study can help to overcome the shortage of photosensitizers and provide a new method for PDT/PTT dual-light antimicrobial therapy.

Curcumin was purchased from Aladdin reagent Co. (Shanghai, China) Anhydrous citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Maclean's Biochemical Technology Co. (Shanghai, China). Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli were purchased from Luwei Technology Co. (Shanghai, China). Agar and broth were purchased from Luqiao Technology Co. (Beijing, China).

Carbon dots were synthesized by microwave method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials. In short, 3.0 g of citric acid was dissolved and diluted with pure water to prepare a citric acid solution with a 12 % concentration. 5 mL of 12% citric acid solution and 470 μL of ethylenediamine were added to 5 mL of pure water, dissolved by microwave reaction with pure water, dialyzed, and lyophilized to obtain carbon quantum dots.

To synthesize CDs-Cur, carbon quantum dots solution and curcumin solution (1 mg/mL) were homogeneously mixed at v/v=2:1, stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The mixture was dialyzed and lyophilized to obtain CDs-Cur nanocomposites.

CDs, Cur and CDs-Cur were prepared at 0.1 μM, and the absorption spectra (scan range 200 nm-800 nm) were measured by UV spectrophotometer; the changes of fluorescence intensity of the samples were analyzed by measuring the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths using fluorescence spectrometer; the dried potassium bromide was mixed and ground in the ratio of 100:1 with the samples, and then the infrared spectra of the samples were measured by infrared spectrometer The samples were mixed with potassium bromide in the ratio of 100:1, and then the infrared spectra were measured by infrared spectrometer.

Chemical capture spectrophotometry is one of the most commonly used methods for the determination of singlet oxygen, 1,3-diphenylbenzofuran (DPBF) was chosen as the singlet oxygen capture agent. The DPBF master batch was prepared with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) at a concentration of 5.0 × 10−5 mol/L. 36 μL of DPBF master batch was added to 1.5 mL of the sample solution that had been ventilated with oxygen for 10 min and mixed well. The solution was irradiated with a 405 nm laser at a power density of 20 mW/cm2 and the absorption spectrum was measured after irradiation. Using water as the blank, the procedure was the same as above, and the whole procedure was operated under protection from light.

Cur and CDs-Cur were conFig.d with saline at different concentrations, and 90 μL of sample solution was added to 10 μL of bacterial suspension (1 × 108 CFU/mL) and incubated at 37°C for 30 min. After light or non-light treatment, 90 μL of nutrient solution was added to 10 μL of bacterial solution under the same conditions and incubated for 30 min as a negative control group. On the ultra-clean bench, the solutions of the above groups were diluted and evenly spread on nutrient agar plates and incubated in the incubator at 37°C for 24 h. The number of colonies in each plate was counted by plate counting method. The bacterial inhibition rate of Cur, CDs-Cur against S. aureus /E. coli was calculated.
Bacterial survival rate% = (number of colonies in the experimental group / number of colonies in the negative control group) × 100 %
All experiments were performed in 3 replicates. In the antibacterial experiment, the power density of near-infrared 808 nm and visible light 405 nm was 500 and 200 mW/cm2, respectively.

0.1 mL of CDs, Cur and CDs-Cur solutions was taken in centrifuge tubes (1 μM), respectively, and the samples were irradiated with combined light (808 nm+405 nm; 500 mW/cm2) and visible light (405 nm; 200 mW/cm2) for 40 min, respectively, and the temperature changes were measured with a digital thermal probe. The repeated temperature rise curves of CDs-Cur irradiated with 808 nm light source was measured.

The appropriate amount of mouse eye blood was extracted and kept in a test tube containing sodium citrate, and the erythrocyte suspension was separated by centrifugation and conFig.d with physiological saline into 2% erythrocyte solution. CDs-Cur or Cur sample solution was mixed with 2% erythrocyte suspension in a 1:1 ratio, so that the final concentrations of Cur were 10, 5, 1, 0.5, 0.1 μM. 2% erythrocyte saline suspension was used as the negative group, and 2% erythrocyte pure water suspension was set as the positive group. The mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min in a water bath at 37°C for 1 h. The absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 545 nm. The hemolysis rate was calculated as follows:
Hemolysis % = (sample absorbance −negative control absorbance) / (positive control absorbance− negative control absorbance) × 100 %

Composites cytotoxicity was evaluated to investigate the effect of CDs-Cur on the survival of human normal hepatocytes (L0-2). Cell suspensions (200 μL) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1640 nutrient solution were added to 96-well plates and incubated at 37°C and 5% CO2 in an incubator for 24 hours. Then, cells were incubated with CDs-Cur (final Cur concentrations of 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μM), respectively, at 37°C and 5% CO2 for 24 hours. CCK-8 (5%, 200 μL) was added to each well and after 2 h of incubation, the absorbance of the solution at a wavelength of 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (Epoch2, Bio Tek).

Carboxyl groups of citric acid firstly polymerized with amine or hydroxyl groups to form amide or ester bond, which are excellent precursors for the preparation of carbon quantum dots by pyrolysis [24]. The microwave-assisted heating method can dehydrate and carbonize small molecules, and is a "bottom-up" method to prepare carbon dots, which is simple to operate and does not require high reaction conditions. From the transmission electron microscopy images, it can be seen that the CDs prepared by the microwave method are spherical particles with relatively homogeneous dispersion and no obvious aggregation, and the particle size is between 6-8 nm, which is in accordance with the literature reports on carbon quantum dots.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the aqueous carbon quantum dot solution is yellow and the aqueous carbon quantum dot solution fluoresces bright blue under UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm. The UV spectrum showed a strong absorption peak of CDs at 350 nm, which was due to the n-π* electron leap of CDs. The characteristic peaks of Cur appeared near 265 and 425 nm, respectively, which was the strong K-band absorption of benzene ring and long chain conjugated system. The appearance of the characteristic absorption peaks of CDs and Cur in the UV spectrum of CDs-Cur without significant shifts indicated that CDs and Cur had combined to form a composite system of CDs and Cur.

From Fig. 2B, the maximum excitation wavelength of CDs was found to be 360 nm, and the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum, the excitation wavelength of CDs at 300-400 nm did not show red-shift phenomenon, which was different from the fluorescence characteristics of most of the carbon quantum dots, as reported in the literature. It may be because the prepared carbon quantum dots have uniform particle size distribution, and the carbon dots of the same particle size are excited at different excitation wavelengths. In Fig. 2C, the fluorescence of the same concentration of CDs-Cur was significantly weakened at the same excitation wavelength, and curcumin is an acidic polyphenol, and some studies showed that curcumin can significantly burst the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and there may be an interaction force between CDs and Cur that makes the fluorescence intensity of the system weakened [30].

In the IR spectrogram (Fig. 2D), CDs contain several characteristic absorption peaks, among which the broad peak appearing at 3380 cm−1 was attributed to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, 1658 cm−1 was due to C=O stretching vibration, 1558 cm−1 was attributed to be N-H stretching vibration and 1294 cm−1 was presumed to be C-H stretching vibration, and these functional groups proved that carbon quantum dots had superior water solubility. The absence of 1800-1650 cm−1 peaks in the IR spectrogram of natural photosensitizer curcumin was attributed to the keto-enol interconversion isomeric structure of curcumin, 1508 cm−1 was presumed to be the C=O stretching vibration of ketone group, 1027 and 854 cm−1 were due to the C-O-C stretching vibration of curcumin. The characteristic peaks of curcumin also appeared in the CDs-Cur infrared spectrogram, and the peaks at 1583 cm−1 and 1272 cm−1 were attributed to N-H and C-H, indicating the successful complexation of CDs with Cur.

1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used as a ROS trap. DPBF is a single-line oxygen quenching medium that was quenched by single-line oxygen generated by CDs and Cur, resulting in a decrease in absorbance at 416 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 , the absorbance of pure water remained essentially unchanged, indicating that pure water did not produce singlet oxygen for DPBF under irradiation with a 405 nm light source. Compared with Cur, while the absorbance of CDs-Cur showed more significant decrease at 416 nm, and the degradation rate of CDs-Cur on DPBF was faster than that of the control Cur (Fig. 3d), indicating that CDs-Cur was able to produce more single-line oxygen. After 9 s of irradiation, the degradation rate of CDs-Cur reached 80% twice that of Cur, and the combination of the photosensitizer with carbon quantum dots did not affect the nature of the photosensitizer itself, which could produce singlet oxygen to meet the photodynamic therapy demand.

To meet the needs of combined PTT and PDT treatment, it is necessary to investigate whether CDs-Cur has photothermal conversion effect under dual-wavelength irradiation. Therefore, in the photothermal performance test, dual wavelengths (405+808 nm) under the same conditions were selected to irradiate the same concentration of Cur and CDs-Cur, and their photothermal effects were explored by plotting their temperature change curves with time.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the temperature rise of CDs-Cur and Cur was not obvious under the irradiation of 405 nm light source, while CDs-Cur and Cur could generate heat under the irradiation of 808+405 nm light source, and the temperature of CDs-Cur warmed up from 30°C to 62°C around 10 min, which was caused by the radiative heat of near-infrared light. The results showed that CDs had the property of photothermal material and can produce photothermal heat well under 808 nm light source, so that the loaded Cur can produce photothermal effect better. As seen in Fig 4B, the overall temperature of CDs-Cur solution under 808 nm light source, warming and cooling did not decrease under three cycles, demonstrated CDs-Cur had good photothermal treatment effect and photostability. The above results demonstrated the photothermal effect of CDs-Cur and its potential as a PTT reagent.

Gram-positive S. aureus was used as a representative model to determine the antibacterial ability of the samples. Different concentrations (0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 nM) of Cur and CDs-Cur were mixed with S. aureus suspensions and incubated for 30 min, given 40 min of different wavelengths of light and incubated for 24 h before counting the number of plate colonies.

As shown in Fig. 5 , both Cur and CDs-Cur at low concentrations had some dark toxicity to S. aureus under non-light conditions, but there was no significant change in the survival rate of S. aureus administered in this concentration range. CDs-Cur at 1 nM, 10 nM and 100 nM inhibited 90%, 99.9% and 100% of S. aureus, respectively, under the irradiation of 405 nm light source, while the free Cur at the same concentration only reached the highest 90% inhibition rate against S. aureus. In addition, CDs-Cur at concentrations as low as 1 nM was able to kill 100% of S. aureus under 808 nm + 405 nm light source irradiation, while a concentration of 100 nM was needed to reach 100% bacterial killing rate for pure Cur.

As shown in Fig. 5, both low concentrations of Cur and CDs-Cur had no significant antibacterial effect on E. coli under light conditions. CDs-Cur at 1 nM and 100 nM annihilated 99% and 99.9% of E. coli, respectively, under 405 nm light source irradiation, while E. coli remained alive when the Cur concentration reached 100 nM. In the 808+405 nm light source irradiation, CDs-Cur with concentration of 10 nM killed 100% of E. coli, while Cur with the same concentration killed only 90% of E. coli. These results showed that the antibacterial performance of CDs-Cur was significantly stronger than that of Cur against S. aureus and E. coli under light source irradiation,.

The biocompatibility of CDs-Cur composites was evaluated by hemolysis analysis. As shown in the Fig. 6 A, hemolysis experiments showed that the hemolysis rate increased slightly with increasing concentrations of Cur and CDs-Cur, and remained below 5% when the concentration was as high as 10 μM (hemolysis rate higher than 5% is not suitable for clinical use), and the hemolytic activity of CDs-Cur on erythrocytes was negligible. The cytotoxicity of the nanocomposite was evaluated by investigating effect of CDs-Cur on the cell viability after incubation with human normal hepatocytes at 37°C for 24 h (Fig. 6B). Although the CDs-Cur concentration was several times higher than that used for bacterial inhibition, the cell viability remained high (≥80%), indicating that the nanocomposite had no substantial cytotoxicity or adverse effects on mammalian cells, suggesting that the composite had good biocompatibility.

In summary, the experimental data showed that the carbon quantum dots were synthesized by citric acid and ethylenediamine using a relatively fast and simple microwave synthesis method, and successfully loaded with curcumin and construct the composite nanomaterial CDs-Cur. The prepared carbon quantum dots are uniformly dispersed and have excellent water solubility, fluorescence properties, etc. The large amount of reactive oxygen species and photothermal properties indicated that the composite can produce under 405+ NIR 808 light source, so the PTT /PDT synergistic effect of CDs-Cur in antibacterial experiments significantly improved the photoinactivation efficiency of free curcumin against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Finally, the results of biocompatibility experiments showed that the composites possess a certain safety level and did not affect normal cells. Therefore, CDs-Cur has a bright future as a novel photosensitizer in combined phototherapy for antibacterial treatment.

Fangzhou Wen: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Formal analysis. Peiyuan Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Hongrong Meng: Formal analysis. Hongjun Yan: Validation, Methodology. Xiaodong Huang: Methodology. Hao Cui: Validation. Wei Su: Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51961009), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangxi Zhuang autonomous region (Gui ke AD1924509), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2018GXNSFAA281345), “BAGUI Scholar” Program of Guangxi Province of China, GuangXi Distinguished Expert Project (Standard Research of Zhuang and Yao Ethnic Medicine, GUI Talent Tong Zi (2019) No. 13), Guangxi Key Laboratory of Zhuang and Yao Ethnic Medicine.
